title: Using nitrogen activators to speed a slow or cold compost heap
subtitle: Comfrey, nettles, and other activators: what they contribute and how to apply them
excerpt: A slow compost heap usually lacks nitrogen. Adding a high-nitrogen activator restarts microbial activity and raises temperature within days. Comfrey leaves, fresh nettles, chicken manure, and urine are all effective. This covers how each works, the quantities to use, and where they fit in the C:N balance.
sourceNotes: HDRA/Garden Organic research on comfrey in composting. Lawrence and Newell NPK analysis of organic activators. RHS guidance on compost activators.

glossaryTerms.definition: The ratio of carbon to nitrogen in composting materials. The optimal range for active decomposition is 25:1 to 30:1.
A composting method that reaches 55 to 70°C through active microbial decomposition.

content.text: Most slow or cold compost heaps are short of nitrogen. The
has drifted too high because more browns (cardboard, dry leaves, straw) have been added than greens (fresh grass, kitchen scraps). The microbes responsible for
need nitrogen to build protein. Without it, the carbon breaks down slowly and the heap stays cold. Adding a nitrogen source raises the temperature within 2 to 5 days.

Fresh comfrey leaves (Symphytum officinale or the sterile Bocking 14 cultivar) have an N content of 2 to 3% by dry weight and a C:N ratio of around 10:1. They are among the most effective garden-available activators. A double handful of fresh comfrey leaves laid in a layer between compost additions, or forked through an existing slow heap, adds a concentrated nitrogen dose. Comfrey also contributes potassium and phosphorus. For a 1 m³ heap that has stalled, one or two buckets of fresh comfrey leaves mixed through the top layers is sufficient.

Fresh nettles have an N content similar to comfrey. They are available in large quantities in spring and early summer. The same application rate applies: one or two buckets of wilted fresh nettles, mixed or layered through the heap. Nettle leaves wilt quickly and break down rapidly; they are a quicker-acting activator than comfrey because of their smaller cell structure. Wear gloves when handling nettles.

Fresh chicken manure has an N content of around 4 to 6% by dry weight, the highest of common farmyard manures. The C:N ratio is approximately 7:1 to 10:1. A shovel-full or two of fresh droppings added to a stalled heap is a powerful activator. Pelleted chicken manure (sold as organic fertiliser) at 100 to 200 g per layer acts as a slower-release activator; it dissolves over 1 to 2 weeks in a moist heap.

Fresh urine is approximately 0.5% nitrogen (as urea), with a C:N ratio of near-zero. It is free, immediately available, and effective. Dilute 10:1 with water and pour 5 to 10 litres into a stalled heap. Or use undiluted directly on a dry heap that also needs moisture. Urine is sterile when produced; it carries pathogens only from a host with a urinary tract infection. Normal precautions apply in vegetable plots: do not apply undiluted to edible leaves.

Commercial compost activators sold as granules or liquids typically contain sulphate of ammonia or blood meal. Blood meal has an N content of 12 to 14% by weight and is the most concentrated garden-available activator. A handful (50 to 100 g) per layer is sufficient. It dissolves quickly and smells briefly (an ammoniacal note for a day). Avoid large overdoses of high-nitrogen activators: exceeding a C:N of 25:1 in the opposite direction (too much nitrogen, not enough carbon) causes ammonia release and an unpleasant smell.
Check the heap moisture: it should be damp (wrung-out sponge). If dry, wet it first, then add the activator. Dry heaps cannot process any nitrogen input.
Add the activator in layers or forked through the heap rather than dumped on top. Mixing ensures the nitrogen contacts the carbon throughout the pile.

Take a temperature reading 48 hours after adding the activator. A successful activation raises the temperature by 10 to 20°C above ambient. If the temperature has not risen, the heap may also need turning to introduce air.
